input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Infrastructure assets in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, specifically buildings, characterized by integration of static models with real-time operational data to enable simulation, analysis, and optimization across the entire building lifecycle from design to operation phases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides real-time monitoring insights, predictive analytics for maintenance and operational optimization, simulation, analysis, and enhanced decision-making capabilities for data-driven infrastructure management across the building life cycle, targeting AECO stakeholders including architects, engineers, and facility managers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs real-time monitoring and live synchronization between physical assets and their digital counterparts, with continuous data exchange to support immediate insights and decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated via a model-driven framework with modular architecture, integrating Eclipse Hono and Ditto for IoT data management and leveraging Unreal Engine for high-fidelity visualization to ensure seamless component integration and system-wide coordination.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integrates external systems through standardized frameworks, enabling bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets using Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and interoperability with enterprise systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: BIM-based geometric models enriched with Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) defined by Building Control Engineers. Data sources include physical building sensors and actuators, managed via real-time data exchange for synchronization. Model transformations use Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations and generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modeled by Building Control Engineers within BIM, with endpoint addresses extracted as metadata during MDE-based transformation. These addresses enable precise addressing and remote control of physical actuators through standardized communication protocols. Specific actuator types, operational ranges, and safety constraints are not explicitly detailed in the provided document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined by Building Control Engineers in BIM, with endpoint addresses extracted as metadata for device addressing. Data collection involves real-time transmission of sensor readings via standardized protocols, though specific sensor types, sampling frequencies, and accuracy metrics are not detailed in the provided document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts through bidirectional data exchange facilitated by the DT Framework, with interactive visualization providing immersive interfaces for monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Modular architecture with standardized extension points supports integration of custom services and analytics, enabling DTs to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime configuration management allows adjustments without requiring full redeployment, addressing scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems and maintainability through low-overhead updates.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers, Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation to generate deployable artifacts, real-time data exchange for synchronization with physical systems, interactive visualization for Facility Manager operations, and evolution through DT Engineers integrating custom services via modular extensions. Lifecycle governance is structured across stakeholder roles with automated phase transitions.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process utilizes Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, enabling adaptation to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical and digital systems, with interactive visualization providing immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. Custom analytics services integrated via modular architecture support operational decision-making processes.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework implements a modular architecture with standardized extension points to enable integration of custom services and analytics, though no specific industry standards, compliance frameworks, or interoperability specifications are detailed in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the system as infrastructure assets in the AECO domain (specifically buildings) and details the integration of static models with real-time data for lifecycle optimization. This is strongly supported by the source text. Quotes: 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain', 'virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization', 'complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The extraction correctly identifies the services provided, such as real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, simulation, and decision-making support. It also correctly identifies the target stakeholders (architects, engineers, facility managers) and the scope (data-driven infrastructure management). Quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities', 'improved stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life cycle'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists specific tools and technologies used for the Digital Twin implementation. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', and 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic is highly specific and accurately reflects the source text. It correctly identifies the use of 'BIM-based geometric models' and the enrichment with 'Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers)' defined by 'Building Control Engineers' (Section III-A). It accurately cites the data sources as 'physical building sensors and actuators' and the management approach as 'real-time data exchange' using Eclipse Hono and Ditto (Introduction). Finally, it correctly states that 'Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles' are used to 'extract structured representations and generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention' (Section III-A, Introduction).

The extraction precisely captures the modeling workflow, noting that actuators are defined in BIM by Building Control Engineers and that endpoint addresses are extracted for remote control. It correctly identifies the absence of specific hardware details in the text. Supporting quotes include: 'Building Control Engineers... define... actuators... directly within the BIM environment' and 'extract relevant metadata (e.g., tenant and device ID or endpoint addresses)'.

The extraction is accurate and grounded in the text, describing the definition of sensors in BIM, the extraction of addressing metadata, and the reliance on standardized protocols for real-time data. It correctly notes that specific technical specifications like sampling frequencies are absent. Supporting quotes: 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment' and 'continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the architecture, integration, and automation of the DT deployment process. While it mentions a validation step for the input model ('after validating the model' in Section IV) and evaluates the DT using the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric', it does not discuss model fidelity requirements, uncertainty quantification, or error detection/correction mechanisms as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, the specific details regarding fidelity and validity considerations are indeed not present.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

While 'governance' is mentioned in the context of Model-Driven Engineering encoding organizational policies, the text specifies this relates to 'architectural decisions' and 'quality assurance' rather than data ownership, privacy protection, or consent management.

The source documents describe the technical integration of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and mention extracting metadata such as 'tenant and device ID or endpoint addresses'. However, the text does not detail specific cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, or risk assessments.
